nimbleModel
  • Home

Implementation (Perry’s notes)

This document contains Perry’s notes on the new nimbleModel concepts and implementation after talking to Chris. (Some text is freely copied from the current manual/implementation.Rmd)

Terminology:

  • A “slot” refers to one index entry. E.g. in x[5, 2:8, j], there are three slots, filled by 5, 2:8 and j, respectively.

Classes representing the most basic indices and variables

  • An indexRange represents the index context of one indexing slot (or multiple slots for an indexRangeMatrix).

    • Possible new name: indexSet (there is no ordering implied).
    • Examples: 5, 5:10, and c(5, 10, 8)
    • These correspond to subclasses for scalar, sequence, and matrix (i.e. arbitrary) ranges, respectively.
    • Possible addition: When a model uses x[k[i]], and k is constant, it may be helpful to have an indexing vector k with start and end indices. A goal would be to minimize copying of k through many processing steps and instead have it live in one object used by reference.
    • Use case: In a call like getDependencies(x[1:10]), the 1:10 of the input is an indexRange.

  • An indexRangeMatrix is a special kind of indexRange that represents the index content of multiple indexing slots when they are inextricably tied together.

    • Possible new name: indexSetMatrix.
    • Example: c(5, 10, 8) in one slot and c(7, 5, 3) in another slot.
    • Use case: Say we have model code for(i in 1:3) x[k[i], l[i]] ~ dnorm(0, 1) with constants k and l being the vectors just given in the Example. The set of indices for x are the inextricably linked pairs [5, 7], [10, 5], and [8, 3].

  • A varRange represents a variable and a full set of index contents.

    • Possible new name: varSubset or varIndexedSet or indexedVar

    • It contains a variable name, one or more indexRanges, slot index (or indices) for each indexRange, and coding for how each indexRange is crossed or combined with others to represent the set of variable elements correctly.

    • Ultimately we may want to be able to collapse multiple varRanges to a single varRange when possible.

    • Derived classes: nodeRange

    • Not derived classes: calcRange

    • Use cases:

      • In a call like getDependencies(x[1:10, 5:8]), the x[1:10, 5:8] is a varRange with two indexRanges. It has nothing to do with the content of x or graph relationships of x. It is just an encoding of the symbols x paired with 1:10 (first slot) and 5:8 (second slot).
      • Return values from many kinds of model-structure queries may be varRanges.

Classes representing indices and variables with enhanced content or methods

  • A nodeRange represents a varRange that is a set of nodes

    • Possible new name: nodeSet or varNodeSet or varNodeSubset

    • It is a derived class of varRange that knows which indexRanges are external (i in y[i,1:3]) and internal (1:3) in y[i,1:3]).

    • Example: Say we have for(i in 1:3) {y[i, 1:3] ~ dmnorm(...)}, resulting in three vector nodes with y. A nodeRange for y[1:2, 1:3] contains a set of nodes and knows that they are y[1, 1:3] and y[2, 1:3]. In contrast, a varRange for y[1:2, 1:3] represents an arbitrary chunk of y that may or may not be nodes. In this example, y[1:2, 1:2] could be a varRange but not a nodeRange (because it is not a set of complete nodes).

    • Use cases:

      • Various API calls (e.g., getNodes, getDependencies) will work with either varRanges or nodeRanges transparently to the user.
      • Sometimes we will want nodes that belong together such as having equivalent topological sorting position. Such “in some sense equivalent” nodes will be grouped into a nodeRange.

  • A calcRange represents everything needed to do calculations (and can actually run those calculations) for a set of nodes from the same declaration (and hence in the same variable).

    • Possible new name: nodeCalcSet or nodeCalcRange.

    • This does not inherit from nodeRange or varRange.

    • This contains all the information needed for actual calculations, including:

      • The declRule that contains the calcFun to be called for actual calculations for a single set of (for-loop) index values (e.g. i = 2, j = 4).
      • Index values from the original declaration context, i.e. the for loop indices.
      • e.g., a declaration of y[i+1] ~ dnorm(x[i], 1) with i=1:5 has calcRange indexing of 1:5 since nodeFunctions use i directly.
      • The sortID (topological sort index)
      • A generic method calculate that loops over ranges of original indices and calls declRule$calcFun for each set of scalar index values.
      • Possible revision: Rearrange where this calculate lives and whether it is a method of calcRange or a function that uses a calcRange.
      • In practice, calcRanges will be grouped not only by being from the same declaration (a requirement) but also by having the same topological sort position (not necessary for the calculation code to work, but necessary to organize calculations in sorted order).

Classes representing mappings from indexRange to indexRange.

  • An indexRule maps one indexRange to another.

    • Possible new name: indexMap or indexSetMap etc.

    • Example: Say we have for(i in 1:5) y[i+1] <- 2*x[i]. An indexRule would determine indices of the LHS (y) from indices of the RHS (x). For input 2:4 we would get output 3:5.

    • By reversing the order of a declaration, we can create both parent and child rules.

    • Inputs and outputs are indexRanges.

    • Kinds of indexRules are implemented as derived classes. Cases include:

      • ‘all’: Consider for(i in 1:5) for(j in 1:3) y[i, j] <- mu[j]. Then mu[j] is used for all of y[1:5, j] (for each j). Then the input indexRange with empty i returns the output indexRange with i=1:5, i.e. all of the i range.
      • arbitrary
      • block,
      • constant

    • Inputs that are out of range do not produce output

    • Includes checks for going out of range

    • Various possibilities for either input or output include no indexing, constant scalar, constant vector, arbitrary, and block.

    • Sometimes multiple index dimensions need to be solved jointly.

Classes to determine nodes from varRanges.

  • A nodeRule is used to determine what nodes are contained within a varRange

    • Possible new name: nodeMap, nodeFinder, varNodeMap

    • Input is a varRange. Output is a nodeRange.

    • Here “node” means a contiguous regular block of a variable on the LHS of a model declaration.

    • Derived classes of nodeRuleClass include: declRuleClass, calcRuleClass, and rhsRuleClass

    • Somewhat analogous to model$expandNodeNames in current nimble.

    • Implementation details:

      • Has external and internal rules for handling i and 1:3 in y[i,1:3], respectively.
      • Implemented as a special case of graphRules (without the notion of two sides).
      • Indexing is over nodes (see comments in nodeRange)

  • A rhsRule is used to determine what RHS nodes are contained within a varRange.

    • Possible new name: rhsMap, rhsNodeMap, etc.

    • Input is a varRange. Output is a nodeRange.

    • Comment from Chris: It is not currently clear when we need to use a rhsRule.

    • Original explanation: “Produced from ‘original’ rhsRules generated from all RHS terms in a declaration and subsequent modification (via exclude()) of those rules based on all or portions of the RHS appearing in a different LHS rule (a calcRule).”

    • Perry thinks (guesses) this means something roughly like the following:

      • Say we have for(i in 1:3) y[i] <- sum(x[i, 1:10]). The rhs nodes created are x[1, 1:10], x[2, 1:10] and x[3, 1:10].
      • From input x[2:3, 5], the rhsRule would return the nodeRange x[2:3, 1:10], which knows that 2:3 are the node indices and 1:10 are internal to each node.
      • Now say we have also for(i in 2:3) z[i] <- sum(x[i, 1:5]). Now the rhs nodes might be x[1, 1:10], x[2, 1:5], x[2, 6:10], x[3, 1:5], x[3, 6:10]. But this kind of “fracture” situation in current nimble was particularly complicated and it would be helpful to have more explanation of how it is handled in the new nimbleModel.

  • A graphRule is used to determine what nodes are children or parents of other nodes.

    • Possible new name: graphMap, edgeMap etc.

    • “Downstream” means “children” or “dependencies”. “Upstream” means “parents”.

    • For the “downstream” direction, a graphRule represents a set of edges from one (RHS) set of nodes of one variable to the LHS nodes of another variable in one declaration. For the “upstream” direction, the roles of RHS and LHS are reversed.

    • Inputs and outputs are varRanges.

    • Example: Say we have for(i in 1:5) for(j in 1:3) y[i+1, j] ~ dnorm(mu[k[i]], sigma[i, j]). There would be one graphRule from the mu[k[1:5]] nodes and one from the sigma[i, j] nodes. From input varRange such as mu[2:4] (1st graphRule) or sigma[2:4, 2:3] (2nd graphRule), we would get output varRanges of y.

    • Dynamic indexing is handled similarly to as in current nimble – all possible dependencies are included in the rule.

    • Implementation details:

      • This uses a set of indexRules to determine all output indices from all input indices.
      • A graphRule also can contain indexConstraints that don’t map to an output index but need to be enforced, such as y[i] <- x[2]. (More explanation needed, possibly by example. I think this is how irrelevant parts of an input are correctly ignored.)

  • A dataRule TBD

Classes for collections of “rules” for one variable

  • A varRules object manages a set of rules of the same kind for a single variable.

    • Possible new name: varRuleSet, varMapSet, varRuleManager

    • Example: Say there are multiple model declarations with elements of y on the LHS. From each declaration, there is a nodeRule to determine a nodeRange of y from a varRange of y. The varRules contains the nodeRule from each declaration with y on the LHS. It is used to determine all the nodes (represented as nodeRanges) of y from all declarations.

    • A varRules is generic because there are different cases where multiple sets of rules for one variable must be managed and applied. For any varRules, the rules are all of the same type. The set of rules in a varRules can be of type:

      • graphRule
      • nodeRule
      • Any derived nodeRule.

Classes for achieving calculations

  • A calcRule is used to determine calcRange(s?) from a varRange.

    • Possible new name: not sure, but something

    • Input is a varRange. Output is one or more calcRanges. (Chris’ notes indicate one calcRange, but couldn’t it be multiple?).

    • Normally, a calcRange will contain topologically-tied calculations. I think a calcRule might return multiple calcRanges so that calculations can be done in topologically valid order.

    • Example: Say y[1]…y[3] are created by one declaration, and y[4]…y[6] are created by another. (Alternative, they are created by the same declaration but the latter are used differently in the model, resulting in different topological order than the former.) The calcRule could take the varRange y[2:5] as input and return calcRanges for y[2:3] and y[4:5].

    • Implementation details (copied from Chris)

      • Inherits from the nodeRule base class.
      • A calcRule generally represents subsets of nodes containing elements of all the same type (e.g., top, end). (Is this simply a consequence of having the same sort order, per next item? -Perry)
      • An individual calcRule is produced from the elements in a declaration that are tied in terms of their sort order and (are all of the same type).

    • More implementation details (copied from Chris. These ones are hard for me to follow. Could some of this be moved to a more narrative explanation of how model code is processed?)

      • Includes parent/child links. (Perry does not know precisely what this means.)
      • Produced from original calcRule generated by each declRule and subsequent fracturing of those rules (using makeCalcRules, which calls fracture()) based on all graph edges. (Noting that theta[i] ~ dnorm(theta0[i], 1) are not necessarily all latent or all top nodes, as it depends on how theta0[i] are declared; this involve splitting an ‘original’ calcRule when graph processing is done.)
      • everything on LHS starts out as both top and end
      • top found recursively determining which calcRules have stochastic parents using setStochParent.
      • end found by recursively determining which calcRules have stochastic dependents using setStochDep.
      • latent found by what is not top or end
      • sortID (topological sorting) determined by recursion based on walking the final set of calcRules (which have parent/child information) bottom-up (so that all data nodes are set to be tied, given data nodes generally more numerous than parameter nodes).
      • sortID can be a vector in state-space type cases.

  • An originalIndexingRule determines the original for loops indices needed for a calculation.

    • Possible new name: originalIndexMap or sourceIndexRule etc
    • Input and output are varRanges. For the output, the variable name is not used and the indexRanges are for the original for loop index variables.
    • This is handled by the indexRule functionality by making dummy declarations such as w[i,j] <- y[i+j] if the focal declaration is, say, y[i+j] <- x[i] + x[j].
    • Use case: Say we have for(i in 1:3) y[i+1] <- foo(x[i]). In order to calculate y[3], we need to determine that the corresponding i is 2. An originalIndexingRule does this.

  • A declRule is used for actual calculations.

    • Possible new name (this doesn’t seem like a “rule” at all?): declCalc
    • Contains the nodeFunction, calcFun. This contains the actual mathematical computation (e.g. dnorm) of a model declaration.
    • Input to calcFun is a vector of scalar-valued index arguments (e.g., calcFun(idx_vector)) for the values of for-loop indices from the original model code.
    • For a given calcRange, the calcFun will be called once for each idx_vector. Iteration to use all needed values of idx_vector is done by the calculate of the calcRange.
    • It is necessary to determine the original (for-loop) index values to set up idx_vector correctly. The declRule contains an originalIndexingRule to do this. This may be used by the calcRange.

Final model definition

  • A modelDefClass objects represent the model definition, with all of the content above organized for use. The core fields are the downstream and upstream graphRules (collected into varRules objects) and the various nodeRules.

Transient classes for processing model code

  • A modelDeclClass object represents information in a single declaration.

    • This class has some of the same features as our previous BUGSdeclClass.
    • While it is used when a modelDef is created, it is not generally used subsequently.
    • A key component is a declRule, which contains upstreamRules and downstreamRules.
    • These rules are aggregated into varRules within the modelDef and then are no longer needed in the modelDeclClass.

out of place

  • What is an indexConstraint? (It seems simple but would be nice to place explicitly in the organization.)

Integrated examples

for(i in 1:2)
  y[i+1] ~ dnorm(mu[1], 1)
for(i in 3:4)
  y[i+1] ~ dnorm(mu[2], 1)

What happens for getDependencies(mu[1:2])?

  1. mu[1:2] is a varRange with one (sequential) indexRange for 1:2.

  2. There is a varRules with all the graphRules for mu.

    • It contains two graphRules, one from each declaration. They happen to not overlap.
    • Each graphRule contains an indexRule.

  3. The result of applying both graphRules is a nodeRange with y[2:3] and y[4:5] (I am confused about whether this is one nodeRange containing two nested nodeRanges, or a list of nodeRanges, or if they are consolidated into y[2:5])

Then if we want to calculate the result, what happens?

  1. The input is the nodeRange(s) y[2:3] and y[4:5]

  2. There is a varRules with all the calcRules for y.

  3. Applying this varRules returns two calcRanges, one for y[2:3] and one for y[4:5].

    • Each calcRange has a declRule, which it uses to convert indices to original (for loop) indices and call a calcFun.

  4. In order by sortID, each calcRange uses its calculate to iterate over “2:3” or “4:5”, use the originalIndexRule in the declRule to determine the original i as 1:2 and 3:4, and call the calcFun for each original index value.
